questions.question: Define a linked list and explain its advantages over arrays. (5 marks)
keyPoints: A linked list is a linear data structure with nodes connected by pointers, stored in non-contiguous memory.
Dynamic size: Can grow/shrink at runtime unlike fixed-size arrays.
Efficient insertion/deletion: O(1) pointer updates vs O(n) element shifting in arrays.
No memory waste: Allocates memory as needed.

questions.question: Explain the concept of self-referential structures with an example in C. (5 marks)
keyPoints: A self-referential structure contains a member that is a pointer to the same structure type.
Example: struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; };
The 'next' pointer can point to another Node, creating a chain.
This is the foundation for implementing linked lists, trees, and graphs.

questions.question: Compare arrays and linked lists. Discuss their relative advantages and disadvantages with examples. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Arrays: Fixed size, contiguous memory, O(1) random access, O(n) insertion/deletion.
Linked lists: Dynamic size, non-contiguous memory, O(n) sequential access, O(1) insertion/deletion at known position.
Arrays are better when size is known and random access is needed.
Linked lists are better when frequent insertions/deletions occur and size varies.
Memory: Arrays may waste space if not full; linked lists use extra space for pointers.

questions.question: Describe the different types of linked lists with diagrams. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Singly Linked List: Each node has data and a next pointer. Traversal in one direction only.
Doubly Linked List: Each node has data, next, and prev pointers. Traversal in both directions.
Circular Linked List: Last node points back to first node, forming a loop.
Circular Doubly Linked List: Combines circular and doubly linked features.
